summary:
South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC), established in 1995 and chaired by Archbishop Desmond Tutu, was created to address the human rights violations of the [[event:2684|apartheid]] era through a process of restorative rather than purely retributive justice. The TRC offered amnesty to perpetrators of politically motivated crimes who made full public disclosure of their actions, while victims were given the opportunity to testify about their experiences and receive reparations. Over 7,000 people applied for amnesty, of whom roughly 1,500 were granted it.

The commission held extensive public hearings that were broadcast on radio and television, bringing the details of apartheid-era atrocities — torture, assassination, disappearance — into public consciousness in an unprecedented way. Its final report, presented to President Mandela in 1998, held the ANC and other liberation movements as well as the apartheid state responsible for human rights violations. The TRC became an internationally influential model for transitional justice, studied and selectively adapted by post-conflict societies around the world. Its legacy within South Africa was contested: critics argued that amnesty without full accountability left injustice unaddressed, while others credited it with enabling a relatively peaceful transition.
